step.title: Step 4
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='Pressing LEFT to see if cursor moves back left')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 4, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1 has two panels. Top-left has 3 green crosses, bottom-right has 2. MOUSE clicking a green cross selects it (adds G dark-gray ring). RIGHT after selection creates g (gray) markers forming a cross shape at a position to the right - possibly a cursor or movement indicator.
- Goal model: Unknown - need to understand the relationship between green crosses and directional movement.
- Action model: MOUSE selects a cross, directional keys move something (cursor? selection?).
- Plan: Test more directional movement to understand the pattern, then figure out the goal.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 3 of about 32 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The g markers didn't move after the second RIGHT press. They stayed at the same position. This means RIGHT only moved once from the selected cross to the next empty cell to the right, and pressing it again didn't move further.
Let me check what changed between the two RIGHT presses:

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed between the two RIGHT presses
before = last_transition.before_frame
after = last_transition.after_frame

before_ascii = before.ascii.split('\n')
after_ascii = after.ascii.split('\n')

print("Changes between two RIGHT presses:")
for r in range(64):
    b_line = before_ascii[r]
    a_line = after_ascii[r]
    if b_line != a_line:
        diffs = []
        for c in range(64):
            if b_line[c] != a_line[c]:
                diffs.append((c, b_line[c], a_line[c]))
        print(f"  Row {r}: {diffs}")

# Check node counts
before_seg = before.segmentation
after_seg = after.segmentation
print(f"\nBefore nodes: {len(before_seg['nodes'])}, After: {len(after_seg['nodes'])}")

# Check g/G nodes before vs after
before_g = [(n['id'], n['color'], n['pixels'], n['hash']) for n in before_seg['nodes'] if n['color'] in ['G', 'g']]
after_g = [(n['id'], n['color'], n['pixels'], n['hash']) for n in after_seg['nodes'] if n['color'] in ['G', 'g']]
print(f"Before g/G nodes: {before_g}")
print(f"After g/G nodes: {after_g}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Only row 0, col 2 changed from W to w - that's just the HUD/timer changing. The game state didn't change at all between the two RIGHT presses. So the first RIGHT moved the cursor/selection to the next cell, and subsequent RIGHT presses don't do anything more.
Let me try LEFT to see if it moves back, and also try other directions. I should also try ACTION7 to see what it does.
Let me also think about the grid structure more carefully:
- The first green cross is at cell column 1 (center col 18.5)
- The g markers are at cell column 3 (center col 30.5)
- Cell column 2 has the second green cross (center col 24.5)
So RIGHT skipped over the cell with a green cross and moved to the next empty cell. This suggests the cursor moves to the next available empty cell in the direction pressed.
Let me test LEFT to see if it goes back to the original position, and try other directions.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Only HUD changed, game state same. RIGHT only moves once.
# Let me test LEFT to see if it goes back, and also try ACTION7

# First try LEFT
predict(expect_change=True, note='Pressing LEFT to see if cursor moves back left')
action(['LEFT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After LEFT:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['G', 'g']:
        center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']), sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']))
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, center={center}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
After LEFT:
  Node 13: color=G, pixels=20, center=(19.5, 18.5)
  Node 20: color=g, pixels=2, center=(18.0, 30.5)
  Node 27: color=g, pixels=2, center=(19.5, 29.0)
  Node 29: color=g, pixels=2, center=(19.5, 32.0)
  Node 33: color=g, pixels=2, center=(21.0, 30.5)
